Policing Amendment is such a bland name for a bill, but it's one of the
biggest grabs of police power in the nation's history.
I believe it's a radical bill, and it's a dangerous bill.
I'm not going to go through our entire submission, but I do want to talk about the

Now, you may be surprised to hear that I'm going to start with police body cameras,

Okay.
What was the allowable purposes this can be done for including safety,

at the NZCCL, we're aware that the Police Commissioner has stated he wants to

So we've been doing some research to work out how we can do it safely.
We're looking at different schemes in other countries, various requirements about
when body cameras can be worn, when they should be turned on, when they can be

The issues are surprisingly tricky, but I believe not insurmountable.
We also believe that the people of New Zealand, through both consultation and the
deliberations of the elected parliament, should have some say in how police
select implement body cameras. This bill just skips past all that
and says the police can record whatever they like.
Pass this law and neither the people nor you MPs will get to say

Furthermore, the bill enables police or police employees in any public
place to use any tool they like in order to record audio or

These tools allow the invasion of people's private places from public

And it allows these tools to be left unattended and running for a long time.
The bill is a green flag to use number plate recognition, facial recognition,

cameras and microphones everywhere. Everything's allowed by this bill.
Now I want to switch to part two,
the road and any other area closure part.
This has expanded a part of the law that police can use to close roads.
It allows them to close any accessible area, which pretty well covers most of the
country.
It also expands the reasons the areas can be closed.
While the puffery at the start of the bill talks about antisocial road use, our
reading is the text that one or more of the following apply means it applies in
all of the other circumstances as well.
It seems the bill will allow police to shut down any area, remove
the people for a number of reasons, including they believe that public
disorder is imminent, or that a group of people may be expected
to cause excessive noise from a vehicle.
Now, these aren't the totality of the reasons listed, but I've picked out these
because firstly, they deal with things that haven't actually happened at the time
the order was made.
As someone who believes that people should be allowed to go about their lives
freely as much as possible, this seems very wrong to me.
Now we not only have to avoid breaking the law, we also have to make sure we don't
give the impression that we might break the law.
The second reason I've picked them out is that those descriptions also apply to
political protest.
Normal political protest in a democracy is often loud, at least
somewhat disorderly, and sometimes involves people using loudspeakers or
music systems mounted on vehicles.
It seems to me the powers in this bill will be able to use to shut down political
demonstrations, order people to disperse, and arrest them if
they refuse or try to return. And this is a big problem for a rights-respecting
democracy.
Now, to finish off, I want to talk about what these two parts have in common,
and what it is is that there are no meaningful
restrictions on the use of these powers. There's no exceptions.
There's no oversight. There's no approval process or requirement for a
senior officer to be involved. There's no appeal.
There are really no limits.
The bill embodies a view that the police will never misuse or abuse their
powers, that every police officer and employee, because it covers employees
as well, will always do the right thing, that we don't need to limit how
police powers are used.
I believe this is a very dangerous view. Police are human.
They make mistakes. They exhibit bias.
Some of them are unfortunately corrupt.
And for one obvious and exhaustively documented example, police
statistics and reports clearly show they're biased against Māori.
A society that enacts laws that the police are always right is going down a very
dangerous path towards authoritarianism, and that's why I say it's a
radical bill. It's a radical extension of police powers, and it's
radical in the way that it assumes that police can do no wrong.
I believe that we should not pass it in its current form.
And that's not to say the purpose of the bill is inherently wrong.
I believe there is room to better codify how, when, where, and
why police can record audio and video.
I believe there may be room to give police the ability to close areas where
large-scale lawlessness is happening.
But these purposes have to be taken seriously in the context of a
rights-respecting democratic society that values freedom,
which is a version of Aotearoa that I'd like to live in.
They need proper consideration of how we can let police do their
job while still protecting our rights, not this carte blanche
do what you want license.
I recommend you send this bill back for a substantial rewrite.
If you don't, please consider some of the suggestions we made in our written
submission. Thank you for your time. I'm happy to answer any questions.

also video that

using long-lens cameras to zoom in and take

issue.
And that applies across all these things.
The actual rules we come up with,
I've got opinions on what some of those should be and so on, but
we do have to consider there's power imbalance, and that's why we need

The New Zealand Law Society, and I think we may have
Juliet Philpott and Nick Chisnell.
Welcome. You have 10 minutes with us today. Thank you.
Yes. Tena koutou katoa. I'm Nick Chisnell KC.
I'm the convener of the Law Society's Criminal Law Committee, and I'm
joined by Juliet Philpott, who's a member of the Human Rights and
Privacy Committee.
I intend to make some preliminary comments before asking Juliet to address you
on Part 1 of the bill. I'll then briefly address Part 2 before

Thank you.
My starting point is that the Law Society joins the Privacy Commissioner and others
who have expressed deep concerns about the significant proposed
expansion to police intelligence gathering powers in Part 1 of
the bill. For Part 2, we query the
justification for and workability of the proposed new powers
to enforce the temporary closure of publicly accessible areas.
If the bill was to proceed, we'd invite greater safeguards
be put in place. So simply put, the Law Society does not
consider that the bill should pass in its current form.
The policy justifications for the extensions that the bill
proposes to law enforcement powers are questionable and have not been
sufficiently articulated.
The lack of public consultation has produced a set of proposals
that risk overreach and which are incompatible with settled privacy
principles and freedom of expression.
In our submission, if enacted, the bill will create real difficulties
in application for police officers tasked with using its powers.
Inevitably, litigation will ensue because the bill does not clarify
the law.
There is a real likelihood that Māori and Pasifika communities will be

to photograph and record for information gathering.
The bill's intelligence gathering powers will have a far greater
impact on the privacy interests of the community at large than any
other kind of information gathering power currently available to
law enforcement agencies, and it stands in stark contrast,
in our view, to the careful approach that was taken to the
enactment of the Search and Surveillance Act through
consultation and routine statutory reviews, which in
our view reflects what ought to happen here.
Similarly, the approach taken in the bill starkly contrasts with the
Criminal Investigations Samples Act, which carefully
prescribes the taking and storage of DNA profiles,
and that Act's fitness for purpose was recently considered by the Law

of personal information. So with those comments in mind, I'll now hand over to
Juliet to address you on Part 1.
Thank you.
Tena koutou katoa. As you've seen, the Law Society has made a range
of specific points about Part 1 of the bill, and I'm going to focus on two of
those today. The first is to emphasize our
view that there are insufficient safeguards in either the bill or the Privacy Act
to mitigate privacy risk caused by the expanded intelligence
gathering powers that the bill would give to police.
And I suggest this can be seen in two specific mechanisms that are proposed by the
bill. The first is proposed Clause
45C, which gives the police very broad powers to collect
intelligence on private property without a warrant, and
regardless of whether the intelligence being collected has any connection to the
reason that the police are on the property in the first place.
No specific safeguards are provided in the bill itself, and it
is unclear what the justification for these provisions are, given both the Tamifuna
decision and the IPCAOPC report involved images
taken in public.
The clause, as drafted, amounts to a significant imposition on
individual privacy on private property,
and the Law Society believes that the development of such a proposal
requires clear justification, appropriate safeguards, and specific and
adequate public consultation.
The second mechanism that I
believe
suggests that there are insufficient safeguards is the provisions that enable
collection of personal information for intelligence purposes in public.
If passed, the bill enables information collection for any lawful purpose connected
with a function or activity of the police.
The safeguard and information privacy principle one in the Privacy Act,
which says that police may only collect personal information that is necessary for
a lawful purpose, will no longer apply.
And in addition, as you're aware, the bill provides that police can collect
intelligence data not only if it will support a police function at the time of
collection, but also if it may support a police
function or activity in future.
This is expressed as a limitation on the collection of intelligence, but
we suggest that this future-looking, highly permissive limitation will
provide only limited protection, in particular, combined with the
removal of the necessity requirement.
Although the expressed intention is that the Privacy Act will continue to provide
protection, as you know, the concept of the purpose of collection
of information underpins most of the key information privacy principles in the
Privacy Act. Given the permissive nature of both provisions in the bill
that I've referred to, and the fact that these provisions will supersede the
information privacy principles, there is a concern that any protection
given by the Privacy Act itself would not be adequate.
The second point I want to emphasize in the time we have
relates to the clarity of the drafting of the provisions.
The departmental disclosure statement explains that the bill is intended
to address uncertainty created by the Supreme Court in Tamifuna,
but the Law Society is concerned that the provisions themselves are drafted in a
way that may create rather than mitigate
uncertainty.
An example is the provision permitting collection of information for an
intelligence purpose connected with a function or activity of the police.
As you know, the police's functions are listed in Section 9 of the Policing

defined anywhere. Clarity around the scope of this provision is
essential in our view, and that's not only to ensure
there's clarity about the impact on individual privacy rights,
but crucially to make sure that frontline police staff understand the
limits of their authority to collect information before collection occurs.
And we recommend at a minimum that these terms are clarified.
Those are the two points I wanted to reiterate in the time available.
I will just pass back to Nick to speak to you briefly about part two.
Thank you.
Thanks, Juliet. The Law Society's main concern is that
whilst part two's framed towards road

is another reason why we say that alongside part

And

which are in paragraph 6.15 and 6.16

show the risks associated with this type of legislation, which

With that in mind, we're here to answer any questions that you may have for us.

and with

regulation, where the minister of the time can make
changes, where do you feel the best place for that would be?
I think the difference is in putting a fence at the top of the cliff or an
ambulance at the bottom. If we can get this right from step one as opposed to step
three or four or five, we've seen how easy it is to pass the buck and kick the can
down the road, and it'll get sorted out in the legislative process.
But if we start with good intention, but also good writing and good clarity, we
don't have to go back and fix these things later.
There's no further questions, but thank you.
The submission was very well laid out. But thank you very much for your time today.
Thank you so much.
Thanks.
Not quite sure if BIPOC,
Legal Collective Aotearoa, are they on-
They're coming up
... coming online?
Hello.
Welcome. I see Elizabeth is. Shaymaa, welcome to the Justice
Committee. We have you for 10 minutes today, but please take us
through your key points of your submission, and please leave some time for the
committee to ask some questions.
Tena koutou katoa. My name is Shaymaa Arab.
I appear before this committee as a human rights advocate and a member of the
Muslim community in Aotearoa and on behalf of the BIPOC Legal Collective
Aotearoa-
Welcome
... an organization creating the law and the communities it most affects.
I'm joined today by Elizabeth McLean, who's completing her PhD in psychology at
Victoria University, with a focus on young Māori and the justice system.
Elizabeth will speak to the psychological dimension of this bill after I've
addressed the legal arguments. So between us, we hope to cover not only what this
bill does to the law, but what it does to people.
As you know, the bill exists because police spent years unlawfully photographing
thousands of people, predominantly Māori teenagers, storing those images in a
national intelligence database with no oversight, no defined purpose, and
no deletion framework. A joint investigation by the
IPCA and the Privacy Commissioner found that.
And last year, the Supreme Court confirmed that it was an unreasonable search and
in breach of the New Zealand Bill of Rights Act.
For us, that should've been a moment of reckoning and reflection, but instead, this
bill proposes to make that conduct lawful by statute, and in doing
so, it authorizes what the court found unlawful.
First, I'd like to talk about the intelligence purpose threshold.
The bill authorizes the collection of audio and visual information where a police
officer considers it will or may support a policing function for, quote-unquote,
"intelligence purpose." That phrase is not defined in the bill, and there's no
requirement for reasonable grounds, no requirement for a connection to a specific
investigation, and no objective standard of any kind.
We already know that the Privacy Commissioner has said that it sets a low bar, and
the information privacy principles under the Privacy Act require that information
is collected for a lawful purpose, that it is relevant, and that the collection is
not excessive. An undefined intelligence purpose that requires only a
police officer's subjective belief is difficult to reconcile with any of those
requirements.
In the Supreme Court, they were clear.
Collecting photographs for speculative future intelligence use without a specific
lawful purpose breached Section 21 of the Bill of Rights Act.
So this bill doesn't really respond to that reasoning. It legislates around it.
And we're asking this committee to recommend an objective, articulable standard,
one that requires reasonable grounds connected to a specific policing function
before information can be collected.
And that's not really a radical ask because that's what the Supreme Court's own
reasoning requires.
Second, which is really important, is about racial bias.
As I mentioned before, this bill didn't arise in a vacuum.
It arose because police were systematically and unlawfully photographing Māori
rangatahi, and that practice was unfortunately institutionally
tolerated for many years. The UPD research program found that
police are 11% more likely to prosecute Māori than New Zealand Europeans for the
very same offense. So this shows us that it's a structural pattern and not an
anomaly. New Zealand does have obligations under international law that are
directly engaged here, and these obligations can't be satisfied by good intentions.
They require structural safeguards, which this bill unfortunately doesn't really
have any. There's no requirement to collect or publish disaggregated
demographic data on who is photographed or recorded.
There's no independent audit mechanism, and there's no oversight body.
There is only the same internal police review that failed to catch the original
conduct in the first place.
If this bill passes without those safeguards, we won't know who's being
targeted. We won't be able to identify discriminatory patterns until the harm is
already done. What's missing is proactive systemic oversight.
Sys- yeah, systemic oversight. An oversight, like an independent body with
authority to audit how these powers are being used across the board with public
demographic reporting, so patterns of discriminatory targeting can be
identified before another generation of rangatahi ends up in a database.
So we are asking this committee to require
mandatory public reporting of this demographic data
and to establish an independent oversight mechanism with real authority to audit
how these powers are being exercised.
The third issue I want to raise is one that received a little attention, but I'm
glad it was mentioned today, and that is on facial recognition.
And this could potentially carry significant long-term risk.
In Tamifuna, the Supreme Court specifically noted that photographs are sensitive
biometric information precisely because they are capable of being
analyzed using facial recognition technology.
As a previous speaker said, this is why photographic data is categorically
different from a name or an address.
Once an image is in a database, it can be run against facial recognition systems
indefinitely. So what that means is you collect today, but you identify, track, and
surveil tomorrow. So we are asking that this committee not create a
statutory collection power, and at a minimum, the bill does need to explicitly
address facial recognition. And we also recommend that the bill explicitly
address where collected biometric information can be shared and with whom,
including under intelligence-sharing arrangements with foreign
governments.This is one, the fourth issue is
one that I believe is really, really important for communities of color,
and one that has received far less scrutiny than part one, and that is on the
closure powers.
As you know, it extends to parks and public spaces on a single police officer's
subjective view that disorder may be imminent,
and that threshold is quite low. For the communities we represent, it's not
abstract. Look at Eid prayers, for example, where this draws thousands of Muslim
community members, families, women, children into public spaces.
These are peaceful, lawful gatherings.
Under this bill, if a police officer formed the view that disorder might be
imminent nearby, that gathering could be declared a closure zone.
Attendees who don't immediately leave could be fined or even face criminal
charges. Those closure zone powers don't exist in isolation.
They feed directly into the surveillance framework I already mentioned.
A gathering declared a closure zone becomes a gathering of interest, and the people
present could become subjects of intelligence collection.
So one power enables another. And I'd like to say that this is not a hypothetical
concern for Muslim communities. The Royal Commission of Inquiry has already stated
that there has been over-surveillance of Muslim communities.
And just recently as well,
over the last three years, another case of a young Muslim who attended a lawful
public protest was contacted by the police, and his social
media was referenced, and that caused a lot of fear amongst the community.
I will pass it on to Lizzie, just because I know
we're running short of time, to talk about the psychological dimension.
Thank you. You've still got another four minutes, so fine. Thank you.
Thanks, Shay. Tena koutou katoa. He uri tēnei nō Waikato,
Tainui, Ngāti Maniapoto me Ngāti Pūkenga hoki.
Ko Elizabeth Kararaina McLean ahau.
Today, I just want to really briefly touch on
what legal arguments alone cannot fully capture, and that's just the impact that
this bill could have on people, the psychological impact.
And we're not talking about in a hypothetical what if scenario
or too far in the future. I'm talking about the impact that we could see
now if this was introduced. I want to speak to this coming from the context
of knowing that we already have young people and marginalized communities
navigating a justice system that does not necessarily treat them
equally. So the evidence available to us on surveillance is clear.
You do not need to be watched
to be affected by watching. When young people know
they are being photographed or are possibly going to be photographed,
and that this data will be stored somewhere, they will change their
behavior. They will avoid public spaces, disengage from the
community, and effectively withdraw from certain aspects of civic
life. We call this the chilling effect, and this is a well-known psychological
phenomenon that arises when individuals or groups modify their
behavior due to a fear of the consequences that may
result from this behavior being observed.
And now this is well documented across multiple jurisdictions and in various
contexts.
So with respect to our rangatahi Māori, we're already dealing with a justice system
that is not designed to support them.
There's substantial evidence illustrating legal cynicism amongst our rangatahi
Māori, and when this belief becomes so entrenched during
adolescence or during
the youngest stages in their lives, it can be incredibly difficult to shift
that way of thinking. This, in turn, will influence how our young
people are engaging with the justice system or justice institutes in
terms of whether or not they're likely to report criminal behavior, whether or not
they're likely to engage in support or even reach out for support,
and whether or not they'd choose to engage with justice institutes at all.
This proposed legislation is a direct result of unlawfully photographing
rangatahi Māori. There's a real psychological cost in place to
being treated as a suspect without cause, particularly during
adolescence, an incredibly important time in one's life for
developing a sense of identity and a real sense of self.
This sort of encounter can be really detrimental to not only how
someone sees themselves, but also how they make sense of their relationships
with authority figures and the law in general.
I believe it should be deeply concerning to all of us that if this bill is
introduced, we would only be further entrenching ourselves in a justice
system that is doing harm to marginalized communities.
But also that we'd be normalizing this idea that government surveillance and
information gathering on these communities is okay.
This bill would be telling our young people in the communities that the law has
taken into consideration the context within which they exist and
decided that surveillance without a divine purpose is an acceptable
way to engage with them. I urge you all to consider not only if this bill
is legally defensible, but also whether or not it is in the best interests,
psychologically speaking, for those communities it's going to affect the most.
Nga mihi nui kia koutou. I'm going to hand back to Shaymaa.
Thank you.
Got that. I'll just kind of end it by saying that the Police
Association today mentioned trust and that there should be trust in police to
handle this appropriately.
I'd really like to touch on that because effective policing does depend on
community cooperation, and it depends on people reporting crime, providing
evidence, and trusting that the law works for them as much as it works on them.
But that trust is already really fragile, and the 2024 Crime
and Victim Survey found Māori have consistently lower trust and confidence in the
justice system than non-Māori. And I recall personally when the
15th of March terrorist attacks happened, there was a lot of effort to try and get
the Muslim community to report cases of hate crimes to police because for years,
there was weakened trust and little confidence that police would work with the
community rather than against them.
So the question for this committee is not only whether this bill is legally sound,
it's whether this actually does make New Zealand safer, and whether we should be
investing in potentially other initiatives to build this trust between the
community and the police. And just finally to close off, I'll be
very honest with this committee. The people that are most impacted and affected by
this bill aren't in this room with us.
They are Māori teenagers, they are children of color, they are whānau at Friday
prayers. They're communities who have learned through experience not to assume that
the law protects them. So I do appear before you as an advocate and as a
member of one of those communities because these things are not separate for me.
They're why this work matters, and why I stand behind every one of those arguments.
So I just really hope that the committee does take these recommendations into
consideration, and that they also oppose this
bill.No, I'm hearing you.
Thank you very much, Shamara Elizabeth.
I just see if there's any questions or good-- I'll probably just
ask you, you did bring up around mandatory
public reporting. Shouldn't it be for
all age groups? Like it should, whatever's done here, and I think you're
advocating obviously around children and pictures.
But
I'd just like a little bit more detail on your thinking there.
So sorry, can you talk about the-
Oh, yeah, the mandatory public reporting you were talking
about.
Oh, yes. Mm-hmm.
Yes. So shouldn't that be for all age groups?
Shouldn't just be for-
Absolutely.
So, my point there, sorry, just to clarify, is that, any data on
intelligence gathering should be reported, shouldn't just be based on one specific
ethnicity or demographic. But that will make it clearer what demographics are
potentially being targeted. But there should be a lot more transparency with
anybody who's being surveilled.
Thank you very much. Thank you for the submission.
Very clear submission, and thank you for your time today.
No, Henry. Thank you.
All right.
Thank you.
Thank you. Our last submitter today is the Child, Children's
Commissioner in person. Claire, welcome.
We have, as a committee, already decided to give you some little bit extra time,
so you're with us for at least 15 minutes, maybe longer if we've got a few
questions, but welcome.
Okay. Thank you, Mr. Chair. I'm
Claire Achmad,
the
Children's Commissioner. I'm the independent statutory advocate
for all children in New Zealand under the age of 18, and all
children and young people under the age of 25 who have been in or who
are in the care and/or custody of the state.
I had very much hoped to have some young people alongside
me today to present to you on this bill.
As you know, it's always my intention as Children's Commissioner to share the
platform that I have as an advocate with children and young
people so that they are supported to directly share their
views with you as decision-makers at the system level.
Unfortunately, despite best efforts, I wasn't able to make that happen today.
However, given how this bill will directly impact
the lives and rights of mokopuna, New Zealand's children and
young people, it is essential that their voices and
views are sought out and understood by you as parliamentary
decision-makers to inform your views and your ultimately
recommendations to the Parliament about this bill.
Ideally, mokopuna should have been consulted on this bill prior
to it landing before the Parliament.
Seeing as that has not occurred, I ask you directly,
collectively as the Justice Committee, that you please seek out
children and young people's views on this bill.
And if I can be of any assistance in doing so, I will-
Yeah, that was gonna be my question
... of course, be happy to assist you, Mr. Chair.
As Children's Commissioner, I oppose this bill because of the
significant concerns that it raises from a
children's rights perspective.
You obviously have my written submission, which goes into more
technicalities and more detail.
Today, with my time with you, I want to highlight the key
reasons for my opposition to this bill so that they are well understood,
and the approaches that I am advocating for from a children's rights
perspective.
From a children's rights perspective, the right to
privacy is central to this bill.
All mokopuna in all circumstances have the right
to privacy, including in their interactions with
police and with other apparatus of the state.
Article 16 (1) of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child,
to which New Zealand is a state party, could not be any
clearer. It reads, "No child shall be
subjected to arbitrary or unlawful interference
with their privacy."
And Article 16 (2) of the Children's Convention further
makes clear that the child has the right to the protection of the
law against such interference.
The right to privacy in police interactions is important
for all people, including children and young people.
This is because we are talking about a significant
exercise of the power of the state.
And in comparison with a child or a young person, that
exercise of power and the imbalance of power is even more
pronounced.
Children and young people may be vulnerable in such instances to
breaches of their rights.
The way in which personal information is collected
from mokopuna by police and how it is
used and retained in the context of the criminal justice
system can have profound negative and
sometimes lifelong impacts for mokopuna beyond the
breaching of their rights.
The information privacy principles enshrined in the Privacy Act
must be upheld at all times, including Principle
One relating to the purpose of collection and Principle Four
about the manner of collection.
I have read the Privacy Commissioner's submission on this bill,
and I am concerned that this bill, in effect, will
override the information privacy principles.
That must be avoided. Principle Four makes absolutely
clear that special care and protections are required when
it comes to the manner of how personal information is
collected from children.
As the committee will well knowIn 2022, the
Independent Police Conduct Authority and the Office of the Privacy
Commissioner undertook this joint inquiry,
and it found that police were collecting personal
information from mokopuna unlawfully.
Crucially, the joint inquiry also found the collection of
personal information was a breach of the rights of
children and young people.
As Children's Commissioner, let me be absolutely clear with you
today.
The findings of the joint inquiry were not an invitation
to pass a law to get around unlawful practice that
police had been engaged in.
Instead, the joint inquiry sent a crystal clear
message
that police practice of collecting personal information,
including from children and young people, must be
consistent with their rights.
This bill goes against those recommendations.
The government is obliged in all instances to uphold the rights of
children under the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child, including
to privacy, non-discrimination, and particular rights when
they are in conflict with the law. Those are protected by Article 40
of the convention.
This includes Article 47, and it says that
states' parties to the convention shall ensure a child's
privacy is fully respected at all
stages of criminal proceedings.
I'm very concerned to see this bill before the parliament
without adequate safeguards to protect children's rights
directly built into it.
As I've noted, there is a significant
power imbalance between mokopuna and police in
situations where police want to collect personal
information from mokopuna, including photographs
of them, videos of them, and sound recordings.
In practice, I am mindful that the collection of
photos, videos, and voice recordings of mokopuna
outside of a current active investigation is also
likely to generate a sense of mistrust and
even conflict with mokopuna, who will understandably, I
think, feel unfairly targeted and perhaps surveilled
by police in these circumstances.
This will be especially harmful for mokopuna Māori.
Collectively, I believe we want trust and
confidence between mokopuna, whānau, and police
to be further built and developed, not undermined.
The powers in Part 1 of this bill are so broadly
framed as they currently stand that they are
inconsistent with New Zealand's obligations under the UN
Convention on the Rights of the Child.
I remind you that as members of Parliament, you are duty bearers
under the Children's Convention, and I implore you to examine
this bill with that in mind.
In my view, this bill must not proceed in its current form,
because to do so will mean a law on our books that
opens the door to police abuse of power,
and in doing so, open that door to the breach of the rights of
children.
This must be avoided. Their right to privacy must be
upheld through ensuring effective and clear
safeguards are in place.
I further note that the bill is inconsistent with the Crown's Te Tiriti o
Waitangi obligations as made clear in the articles of Te
Tiriti. Upholding kāwanatanga requires the Crown
to exercise good governance in respect of the criminal justice system,
including addressing and reducing the disproportionate
representation of mokopuna Māori.
Tino rangatiratanga means mokopuna have the right to retain control
of when their personal information is collected and how it is used.
For mokopuna Māori, personal information is their own taonga,
which the Crown must support them to protect.
For these reasons, there must be high thresholds and
clear parameters and age-specific
safeguards for the limited circumstances when police
can lawfully collect personal information from mokopuna.
The bill currently contains none of these things, which is
why I am so concerned about it.
I also remain seriously concerned that the bill will
disproportionately impact those mokopuna who
already experience significant inequities within the
youth justice system. This includes mokopuna Māori, tangata
whenua Māori in particular.
The joint inquiry of the IPCA and the OPC found that
53% of personal information collected by police
of those under the age of 18 was personal
information belonging to mokopuna Māori.
Pacific mokopuna, mokopuna whaikaha, those with disabilities,
and those with a care experience in the care of the state
also experience inequities within our youth justice system.
I strongly caution against the implementation of this bill in its
current form because it is likely to perpetuate
existing inequities.
If this bill is to progress, I suggest it is
significantly strengthened to safeguard the rights of
children and young people.
I do accept that there are some legitimate
policing purposes to collect and use personal
information from mokopunaSuch as when there is an active
investigation into a crime, or in limited
circumstances when collection is necessary to prevent
harm.
However, I am very clear, this bill should not proceed
in its current form when it comes to a children's rights perspective.
If it is to progress, I strongly recommend that clear
legislated safeguards for children's rights are explicitly
written into the bill to ensure any and all collection
of personal information upholds children's rights.
The situations highlighted in the joint IPCA and
OPC inquiry must be learned from, and
the same mistakes in the exercise and abuse of state
power must be avoided.
The joint inquiry was clear about what these safeguards should
be. And from a children's rights perspective, as I have set
out in my written submission, safeguards should ensure
including, that the information privacy principles
are always applied and upheld when it comes to the rights of
mokopuna, and that police comply with these when seeking to
collect, use, and retain personal information from
mokopuna. Very happy to take any questions.
Thank you. Very strong submission there. Dean Roswell.
Yeah, thank you for that. And just referring to your recommendations, some of
the advice that we've received suggests that children's safeguards can be
largely dealt with through police operational policy
rather than the bill itself. And based on what you've seen in the past,
why is relying on police policy and guidance not
sufficient when children's rights are at stake?
The findings of this joint inquiry make that absolutely
clear.
We simply can't rely on guidance
to ensure that children's rights are upheld.
The fact is that children's rights were breached by the
police in the collection of their personal information.
And so that is why I'm so clear that this bill, if it is to
proceed, must explicitly legislate those
protections into it. There's no excuse to not do
so, and to not do so will go against the
recommendations of this inquiry, and it will go against our
obligations as a nation under the Children's Convention.
So there's an opportunity now to get this right if it is going to
proceed, to make those protections and safeguards
crystal clear within the law so that no one is under
any confusion about the ways in which police
must uphold the rights of mokopuna if they are collecting their
personal information, retaining it, and using it.
Thank you.
Okay, can I just delve into your expertise
around the Children's Convention and internationally?
And can you,
without putting you too much on the spotlight, international examples of where this
is done well?
Hmm. I think that there are examples in other countries
that
we see the Children's Convention having been fully
incorporated-
... into domestic law. So an example of that is in Scotland.
A few years ago, the Scottish Parliament,
I think pretty much by a strong consensus, passed a
law which actually incorporated the Children's Convention
into Scottish law.
That is a really important step that is still open
to New Zealand to do, and I urge that we do go down that
track, because what that means then is that there is no
room for movement in terms of any confusion around
whether or not we should be upholding those rights.
So that
incorporation is a really full-scale way to
ensure children's rights are upheld in a
state.
In New Zealand, we have some partial incorporation of the Children's
Convention. For example, in the Oranga Tamariki Act, some of
the principles of the convention are infused into
that law. And so arguably, New Zealand should always be
upholding children's rights in all circumstances.
But we do see that because we don't have that full incorporation, we
end up in situations like this where laws come before the
parliament that are inconsistent with a children's rights
approach. We want to be avoiding that.
And so in the meantime, before we go to that state of
incorporation,
as a parliament, you have the opportunity to
ensure that explicit safeguards grounded in
children's rights are enshrined into these kinds of laws
that would have a detrimental impact on the rights of children.
Thank you for that. It fully answers that. Carl, did you have anything online? No.
So thank you very much. We took you a little bit over time, but thank you very
much for a very full session today.
Thank you.
Appreciate it.
I'm going to ask anyone that isn't a
official to leave the room and anyone who's working on the
bill with us if they could just remain for a
moment.
Do I need to move to private?
